question_title: Will the interest in “british royal family” change between 2026-05-04 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?
summary: King Charles III's historic state visit to the US (April 27-30, 2026) — the first by a British monarch since 2007 — caused "british royal family" search interest to spike to 100 as of May 2. The visit is now over, and interest will naturally decay following well-documented patterns (half-life 1.5-3.5 days for planned ceremony events). By May 4, interest is still elevated (estimated 25-50 on the 0-100 scale in the resolution window), but by May 16 (16-18 days after peak), it will have returned to near baseline (estimated 5-15). Three Monte Carlo simulations with varying assumptions all show ~99% probability of "Decreases." No major royal events are scheduled for May 5-16. The small residual probability is allocated to unexpected royal news (Increases) and the possibility of extremely fast decay leaving both dates near baseline (Doesn't change).

factors.description: King Charles's state visit to US (April 27-30) caused the spike to 100; visit is now over, natural decay expected over the 12-day comparison window
May 2 was at 100 (peak) in its window — May 4 is only 2-4 days after peak, still significantly elevated above baseline; May 16 will be 16-18 days after peak, back near baseline
Empirical decay patterns show 70-80% drop within first day, half-life 1.5-3.5 days for planned ceremony events (Masicampo & Ambady 2014, Cook et al. 2012)
No major royal events scheduled for May 5-16 that would spike US search interest (only routine garden parties and engagements)
Small chance (~3-5%) of unexpected major royal news (health crisis, scandal) in the May 5-16 window that could boost interest above May 4 levels
If decay is extremely fast (half-life <1 day), both May 4 and May 16 could be at similar baseline levels — but implausible for a multi-day state visit event

reasoning: The premortem approved. The reviewer agrees the direction is clear, noting my 92% is slightly conservative vs simulations (~99%) and their independent estimate (~95%). Let me submit my final forecast.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “british royal family” change between 2026-05-04 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43373)
[State visit by Charles III to the United States - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_visit_by_Charles_III_to_the_United_States)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=british%20royal%20family&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=british%20royal%20family&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16) for 2026-05-16 is within 3 of the value at 2026-05-04. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "british royal family",
  "date": "2026-04-16 2026-05-16",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.

factors.description: King Charles's state visit to US (April 27-30) caused the spike to 100; visit is over, natural decay expected
May 2 was at 100 (peak) in its window - May 4 is only 2-4 days after peak, still significantly elevated above baseline
Google Trends decay patterns show 70-80% drop within first few days of shock events; half-life 1.5-3.5 days for planned ceremonies
No major royal events scheduled for May 5-16 that would spike US interest (only garden parties, routine engagements)
Small chance (~3%) of unexpected major royal news (health crisis, scandal, death) in May 5-16 window that could boost interest
If decay is extremely fast (half-life <1 day), both May 4 and May 16 could be at baseline - but this is implausible for a multi-day state visit event

input.anchor: Base rate for Google Trends topic decreasing by >3 points over 12 days after a confirmed spike: ~80-90% based on decay literature (Cook et al. 2012 - median fame duration ~2 weeks with power-law decay)
input.assessment:
The "british royal family" search interest spiked to 100 due to King Charles III's historic state visit to the United States (April 27-30, 2026) - the first by a British monarch since 2007. The visit included a speech to Congress, state dinner with Trump, and extensive media coverage. May 2 was confirmed as the peak in its window.

In the resolution window (April 16 - May 16), the state visit will be the peak (100). By May 4 (2-6 days after peak depending on exact peak day), interest is declining but still significantly elevated (estimated 25-50 range). By May 16 (16-18 days after peak), interest will have decayed back to near baseline (estimated 5-15 range). The difference of 10-40 points clearly exceeds the 3-point threshold for "Decreases."

Monte Carlo simulations across 3 different model specifications all show 99%+ probability of "Decreases." Even with generous uncertainty about decay rates, new events, and baseline levels, the direction is overwhelmingly clear. The only scenarios that don't resolve as "Decreases" require either: (1) implausibly fast decay (half-life < 1 day) so May 4 is already at baseline, or (2) a major unexpected royal event right before May 16 that exceeds the still-elevated May 4 level.
input.calibration_notes: My estimate of 92% for Decreases is consistent with simulations (99%+) but discounted for model uncertainty and black-swan events. The simulations may overstate confidence because they assume no new major events and well-behaved decay. Real-world probability of Decreases is likely 88-95%.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether there's an unknown upcoming royal event in May 5-16 that could spike interest. 2. Exact Google Trends normalization in the resolution window. 3. SerpAPI data quirks.
input.tool_audit: Useful: mcp__search__web_search (confirmed state visit details, timing, no upcoming events), mcp__research__research (decay pattern analysis was excellent), mcp__sandbox__execute_code (Monte Carlo simulations confirmed directional strength). Empty/failed: research sub-agent couldn't access live news but the web search tools compensated well.
input.process_reflection: The research tool's pre-cached decay pattern analysis was extremely helpful and well-organized. The web search tools quickly confirmed the cause of the spike (King Charles state visit) and the absence of upcoming events. The sandbox simulations were useful for quantifying edge case probabilities. The overall workflow was smooth - this is a relatively straightforward question once you identify the cause of the spike and confirm no upcoming events.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All six factors are grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1**: King Charles's state visit April 27-30, 2026 is confirmed by Wikipedia, CNN, ABC News, White House press release, and multiple other sources in web search results. The question background confirms May 2 = 100.
- **Factor 2**: The question background explicitly states "current value...at the time of writing this question (May 2, 2026)...is 100" in the April 2-May 2 window.
- **Factor 3**: Decay patterns (70-80% drop, half-life 1.5-3.5 days for planned ceremonies) are sourced from the worldview research entry `google-trends-patterns-after-peak-interest-how-qui-b4a50ec8.json`, which cites Masicampo & Ambady (2014), Cook et al. (2012), and empirical Google Trends data. Minor imprecision: research says 70-80% drop "within 24 hours" not "within first few days" as the factor states, but this doesn't change the conclusion.
- **Factor 4**: Web search for upcoming royal events found only Buckingham Palace garden parties (May 6, 8, 12) — routine events unlikely to drive US search interest.
- **Factors 5 & 6**: Forward-looking probability judgments, not factual claims requiring trace support.

### Double-Counting — MINOR (no action needed)
Factors 1 and 3 overlap: both draw on the state visit timing + decay literature to argue for decreases. Factor 1 identifies the causal event and that it's over; Factor 3 quantifies the expected decay rate. They address different aspects of the same underlying phenomenon but share the same evidence base. This inflates the apparent evidence slightly but doesn't change the directional forecast or materially affect the probability.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs match their described direction. Factors 1-4 push toward "Decreases" with positive logits. Factor 5 pushes toward "Increases." Factor 6 pushes toward "Doesn't change."

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
Assessment concludes 92% Decreases, fully consistent with factors overwhelmingly favoring that outcome.

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent correctly identifies: comparison of May 4 vs May 16 values in the April 16-May 16 window, ±3 threshold, SerpAPI as authoritative source, daily precision, UTC dates. The simulation models and assessment directly address these criteria.

### Probability Assessment — MINOR CONCERN (not a fail)
The agent's three Monte Carlo simulations consistently show ~99% for Decreases (99.2%, 99.18%, 99.1%). The agent discounts to 92%, citing "black-swan events and model uncertainty." This is a 7pp conservative discount. The simulations already incorporate: half-life ranges up to 4 days (simulation 3 goes down to 0.8 days), 3% new event probability, baseline uncertainty (3-15), and Gaussian noise. 

Notably, this agent showed the exact same pattern on the prior Swalwell Google Trends question (q43226): simulations at ~96%, initial estimate at 88%, revised to 92% after premortem. The agent appears to have a systematic ~7pp conservative discount on these Google Trends post-spike questions.

My independent estimate based on the evidence: ~95% Decreases, ~3% Doesn't change, ~2% Increases. The 3pp gap from the agent's 92% is not large enough to constitute a fail. The agent's conservatism is somewhat excessive but defensible given no direct access to current Google Trends data for this specific term.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
Anchor is 80-90%. Final probability is 92%, which is 2pp above the top end. This is well-justified: the specific evidence (confirmed massive spike from state visit, confirmed visit ending, known timing 4+ days post-peak, no upcoming events) provides stronger signal than the generic base rate.

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is substantive: identifies three specific failure modes (fast decay making both dates near baseline, unexpected major royal news, Google Trends normalization compression), quantifies thresholds for each, and names concrete scenarios that would change the estimate.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence of 0.72 is appropriate given reliance on decay models calibrated from political figures (not royal family specifically) and inability to access live Google Trends data for this exact term.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent claims "0 errors" which matches ground truth (0.0% error rate). Agent correctly notes research sub-agent couldn't access live news, which is confirmed in the research output ("All web search and news tools were unavailable").

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
The worldview research entries (`google-trends-british-royal-family-search-interest-18ccd73c.json` and `google-trends-patterns-after-peak-interest-how-qui-b4a50ec8.json`) are consistent with the factors. No contradictory forecasts found.

### Pre-Publication Event — N/A
Forward-looking question comparing two future dates.

## Summary
The forecast is well-constructed with thorough evidence. The direction (Decreases) is overwhelmingly supported. The 92% probability is slightly conservative relative to the simulation evidence (~99%) and my independent estimate (~95%), but not enough to warrant a fail. No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, and resolution criteria are properly engaged.
